transcript.text: I'm an automated medical assistant. If this is an emergency, hang up and call 911. Do you consent to continue? (yes/no)
Yes.
What is your main medical concern today?
I have a sore throat.
When did your sore throat start?
2 days ago.
Do you have any fever, chills, or body aches?
I have fever, chills, and body aches.
Any difficulty swallowing or breathing?
No.
Do you have any cough, nasal congestion, or runny nose?
No.
Any swollen lymph nodes or rash?
I have swollen lymph nodes, but no rash.
Have you noticed any white patches or pus on your tonsils or throat?
Yes. I have.
Have you had any prior episodes of sore throat or strep throat in the past?
No. I have not.
How old are you? Please say your age in years.
Do you have any medication allergies? If yes, list them. If no, say no.
Penicillin.
What sex were you assigned at birth? Please say male or female.
Female.
Is pregnancy possible? (yes/no/unknown)
No.
Have you ever been told you have kidney disease or significantly reduced kidney function? (yes/no/not sure)
No.
Have you ever been told you have liver disease (such as hepatitis, cirrhosis, fatty liver, or abnormal liver tests)? (yes/no/not sure)
No.

It sounds like you have symptoms most consistent with strep throat, which is a bacterial infection that often causes sore throat, fever, and swollen glands. Because this is a phone visit, I can’t examine you or do any tests, but based on what you told me, medication treatment may be appropriate. A licensed physician will review this note and, if appropriate, may prescribe an antibiotic that’s safe for you given your penicillin allergy. For now, you can use acetaminophen or ibuprofen to help with pain and fever if you can take them safely. If you develop trouble breathing, swallowing, or if your symptoms get much worse, please seek in-person care the same day. If you experience severe throat swelling or any signs of airway blockage, go to the emergency room immediately. A doctor will review your case and decide on the best treatment next.
